Identity.History: Piracy was a persistent feature of the ancient Mediterranean, thriving wherever weak states, war, and trade routes overlapped. Pirates raided ships, coastal towns, and islands, taking cargo, captives, and slaves. Cilicia, Crete, Illyria, and parts of the Aegean became famous pirate bases. During the late Roman Republic, piracy grew so severe that it disrupted grain supplies and commerce, prompting Pompey’s massive campaign in 67 BC, which suppressed many pirate fleets. Nevertheless, piracy never vanished entirely, remaining a recurring threat throughout antiquity.
